text: The surgeon applies indocyanine green (ICG) dye distal to the tumor for enhanced visualization, with the proximal tattoo marking visible nearby.
The surgeon targets the superior hemorrhoidal artery during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon excises the superior hemorrhoidal artery just distal to the left colic artery in a patient with a long redundant sigmoid colon.
The camera port is positioned in arm three using a twelve millimeter trocar. The ureter is briefly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a twelve millimeter trocar in arm number three, marked for the ostomy, to exteriorize the specimen through the ostomy site.

The surgeon isolates the superior hemorrhoidal artery just distal to the left colic using scissors for dissection. The back end of the artery is cleaned, and a small window is created distal to the left colic take-off, preparing for ligation with a vessel-sealing device.
The surgeon begins the procedure by entering the lateral plane and dissecting along the white line, which is being taken down.

The surgeon begins the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, checking the ureter and using electrocautery on the right side to stay within the mesorectal envelope along the holy plane. The left side is identified, and the surgeon joins the medial and lateral planes to continue the dissection.
The surgeon visualizes the lateral aspect of the left side of the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, focusing on the dissection within the narrow male pelvis.
The surgeon begins the dissection posteriorly, using robotic arm number one to push up and arm number two for retraction and grasping, while circumferentially working down the tissue.
The surgeon examines the interior view, identifying the more proximal ICG tattoo inadvertently placed earlier. The surgeon proceeds to dissect below the peritoneal reflection in a narrow male pelvis.
The surgeon visually inspects the ureter to ensure it is intact and not compromised, noting the absence of stents.
The surgeon scores the peritoneum to connect with the anterior dissection, working distally within the surgical field.
The surgeon manipulates the robotic arms during the posterior dissection, carefully maneuvering to avoid collision and maintain optimal angles for continued tissue separation.
The surgeon observes the very edematous tissues, which appear swollen and inflamed due to radiation exposure.
The surgeon uses a suction device in arm number one to clear smoke and fluid from the surgical field, facilitating a clearer view and speeding up the dissection process.

The surgeon performs anterior dissection, identifying a distal tattoo on the tissue and aiming to dissect a few centimeters below it. The suction device is visible, aiding in clearing smoke and fluid.

The surgeon adjusts the camera position by port hopping to arm two to improve the view, as the right arm number four is obstructed by the iliac. The surgeon prepares to bring the stapler through arm three, continuing circumferential dissection around the targeted area.
The surgeon has switched the camera to arm two, with scissors positioned in arm three and the suction device in arm four, preparing for further dissection.
The surgeon switches to a vessel sealer to grasp and achieve hemostasis while coning in on the mesorectum just above the levator floor.

The surgeon identifies the distal tattoo below the peritoneal reflection and continues dissection to achieve a margin of three to five centimeters below the tattoo, using visual cues to ensure the tattoos are approximately two centimeters below the target area.
The surgeon begins amputating the mesorectum just below the pelvic floor, leaving a small portion of mesorectum for a tumor-specific mesorectal excision.
The surgeon assesses the tumor location, noting it is approximately eight centimeters from the anal verge and four centimeters from the anorectal ring.
The surgeon maneuvers along the levator muscles, approaching the bowel wall to ensure the stapler will staple only the bowel tissue without including any mesorectum.

The surgeon places the stapler from an anterior to posterior approach, ensuring it is positioned below the distal tattoos. The stapling is performed from arm three, providing an advantageous angle. The surgeon checks visually and by palpation to confirm the absence of tumor before stapling.

The surgeon uses a stapler to transect the bowel, followed by scissors to complete the dissection. The anvil is placed through the ileostomy site, ensuring hemostasis while addressing minor bleeding. The ureter is checked to avoid inadvertent damage during the procedure.
The surgeon examines the rectal stump, which retracts into the levator floor following transection. The stapler is used to complete the transection.

The surgeon positions the anvil within the pelvis using the console, then places the stapler from below and mates it to the anvil. The circulator holds the stapler steady while the surgeon releases the robotic arms and fires the staple.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the newly created anastomosis, preparing for a diverting loop due to the patient's radiation treatment and the low position of the anastomosis.
